reasoning: To determine the probability of two consecutive quarters of negative US GDP growth (using initial/advance estimates) entirely within the calendar year 2025, we can start with the historical base rate. Since 2000, this specific occurrence has happened in exactly 3 out of 24 years (2008, 2020, and the false-positive in 2022), implying a base rate of around 12.5%. The exact criteria require the consecutive negative quarters to fall synchronously within 2025 (i.e., Q1&Q2, Q2&Q3, or Q3&Q4). This calendar-boundary strictness excludes recessions split between years (e.g., Q4 2024 & Q1 2025 or Q4 2025 & Q1 2026), naturally knocking down the probability compared to a floating 12-month window. Furthermore, current macroeconomic conditions (robust employment, rising equity markets, and potential Fed rate cuts on the horizon) suggest a 'soft landing' is more widely expected than an imminent severe downturn, keeping the risk of a 2025 recession relatively close to baseline. Thus, a probability close to the historical base rate, slightly adjusted for current economic resilience and the mechanical constraints of the resolution criteria, sits comfortably at roughly 13%.
